title: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)
lesson:
A language model knows a lot, but only what it soaked up during training. It has never read your company's handbook, last week's support tickets, or the PDF you saved this morning. Ask it about those and it will either say "I don't know" or, worse, confidently make something up. RAG — Retrieval-Augmented Generation — fixes this. The idea is simple: before the model answers, you fetch the relevant facts from your own documents and paste them into the prompt. The model then answers using that fresh material instead of guessing from memory.

The open-book exam analogy

Picture two students. The first sits a closed-book exam and must answer purely from memory — that's a plain LLM. The second sits an open-book exam: same brain, but they can flip to the exact page they need before writing. That's RAG. The model's reasoning ability hasn't changed; you've just handed it the right page at the right moment. A second analogy: a chef who has cooked thousands of meals (the trained model) but checks tonight's specific recipe card (the retrieved text) before plating. Skill plus the right reference beats skill alone.

The three steps: index, retrieve, generate

RAG runs in two phases. First, indexing (done once, ahead of time): you take all your documents, cut them into bite-sized pieces — see Chunking — turn each piece into an embedding (a list of numbers capturing its meaning), and store those numbers in a Vector Database (a store built to find the nearest numbers fast).

Then, at question time, retrieval and generation: you embed the user's question, ask the database for the chunks whose embeddings sit closest to it — this is Semantic Search, matching by meaning rather than exact words — and stuff those top chunks into the prompt alongside the question. The model reads the lot and writes an answer. "Augmented" just means the prompt got topped up with retrieved facts before generation.

# Question time: retrieve, then generate
q_vec = embed("How many holiday days do I get?")
chunks = db.search(q_vec, top_k=4)        # 4 nearest passages

context = "\n\n".join(c.text for c in chunks)
prompt = f"Answer using ONLY the context below.\n\n{context}\n\nQuestion: How many holiday days do I get?"
answer = llm.generate(prompt)

Notice the instruction "Answer using ONLY the context". That single line is doing heavy lifting — it tells the model to lean on the pasted facts, not its own foggy memory, which is the main weapon against hallucinations (confident made-up answers).

Why not just fine-tune instead?

People often confuse RAG with fine-tuning. They solve different problems. Fine-tuning bakes knowledge and style into the model's weights by training it more. RAG leaves the model untouched and feeds facts in at the moment of asking. Here's the rule of thumb: fine-tune to change how the model behaves (tone, format, a skill); use RAG to change what it knows (facts that update often).

RAGFine-tuning
Adds fresh facts at question timeBakes knowledge/style into weights
Update by editing documentsUpdate by retraining
Can cite its sourcesCannot easily show where facts came from
Great for changing, private dataGreat for fixed behaviour and tone

Often you use both: fine-tune the voice, RAG the facts.

Where it shines

RAG powers most serious AI products: support bots that answer from your help centre, "chat with your PDF" tools, internal search assistants, and coding helpers that pull in your actual codebase. The reasons are practical. Documents change daily, and editing a file is far cheaper than retraining. You can cite sources, so users (and auditors) can check the answer. And you avoid feeding private data into a training run. One more bonus: it sidesteps the context window limit (how much text fits in one prompt) — instead of cramming a whole 500-page manual in, you fetch only the four paragraphs that matter.

💡 RAG is only as good as its retrieval. If the search hands the model the wrong chunks, even a brilliant model writes a confident wrong answer. Get retrieval right first; the generation usually takes care of itself.

That last point is the whole game. RAG doesn't make the model smarter — it makes sure the smart part is looking at the right page.
mentalModel: RAG turns a closed-book exam into an open-book one: same model, but you slip it the exact relevant pages before it answers. Fetch the right facts, paste them in the prompt, then generate.
mistakes: Treating RAG as a knowledge upgrade for the model. It doesn't make the model smarter or change its weights — it just shows it the right text. If you need new behaviour (tone, format), that's fine-tuning, not RAG.

Ignoring retrieval quality. Most 'the AI is wrong' bugs are really 'we fetched the wrong chunks' bugs. Bad Chunking or a weak embedding model poisons everything downstream. Inspect what got retrieved before blaming the LLM.
Stuffing in too many chunks. Pasting 30 passages 'to be safe' buries the real answer in noise and burns tokens. A focused top 3–5 usually beats a huge dump.

Forgetting to tell the model to stick to the context. Without an instruction like 'answer only from the text below', the model happily blends in its own half-remembered facts, reintroducing hallucinations.
Embedding the query with a different model than the documents. Vectors are only comparable when made by the same embedding model, so a mismatch makes the 'nearest' results meaningless.
Never re-indexing. If a document changes but you don't re-chunk and re-embed it, your bot keeps citing the old version. The index is a snapshot, not a live link.
exercise:
Build a tiny RAG over a single document you know well (a recipe, a rulebook, a README). Split it into ~5 short chunks by hand. Embed each chunk and your test question with the same embedding model, compute cosine similarity between the question and every chunk, and pick the top 2. Paste those 2 chunks plus the question into an LLM with the instruction 'Answer using only the context.' Check that the answer is grounded in the chunks you retrieved.

Stretch goal: Ask a question whose answer is not in the document. Confirm the model says it can't find it (rather than inventing one), then make it cite which chunk number it used for questions it can answer.
solution: The win to look for: when you ask something covered by the text, the top-2 chunks should clearly contain the answer, and the model's reply should echo them — ideally word-for-word on the key fact. The classic failure is the model 'helping' by adding outside knowledge; the 'answer only from the context' line is what reins that in. For the stretch goal, a well-built RAG plus a clear instruction will say something like 'The provided text does not mention that' — that honest refusal is a feature, not a bug, because a made-up answer is far more dangerous. Citing the chunk number ('based on chunk 3') is how real products let users verify, and it instantly shows you when retrieval grabbed the wrong passage.
summary: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) fetches relevant text from your own documents and pastes it into the prompt before the model answers — turning a closed-book exam into an open-book one. You index documents once (chunk, embed, store in a vector database), then at question time retrieve the nearest chunks by meaning and generate from them. Use RAG to change what the model knows (facts, private/changing data) and fine-tuning to change how it behaves. Its weakest link is retrieval: fetch the right chunks and the answer usually follows.

keyTerms.def: A method where you fetch relevant text from your own data and add it to the prompt so the model answers from those facts instead of memory.
The step of searching your stored documents for the pieces most relevant to the user's question.
The one-time prep of chunking documents, turning each chunk into an embedding, and storing them so they can be searched fast.
A bite-sized piece of a document (a few sentences or a paragraph) that gets embedded and retrieved as one unit.
The retrieved chunks that get pasted into the prompt; 'augmented' means the prompt was topped up with these facts before generation.
Tying the model's answer to the retrieved text so it reports real facts and can cite them, rather than guessing.
How many of the nearest chunks you retrieve for each question, e.g. the top 4 most similar passages.
Re-chunking and re-embedding a document after it changes, so the search returns the up-to-date version.
